questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, which case will be invoked when we insert node 65 into the tree?

Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

```mermaid
graph TD
    40((40)) --- 20((20))
    40 --- 60((60))
    60 --- 50((50))
    60 --- 80((80))
    80 --- 70((70))
    80 --- NIL[NIL]
    style 20 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 40 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 60 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 50 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 80 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 70 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```
questions.explanation: Node 65 is inserted as the right child of node 70. Both 65 and 70 are red, creating a violation. The uncle of 65 is the right child of 80, which is a black NIL node. Since 65 is an inner child (right-child of left-child relative to grandparent 80), it triggers **Case 2** (Left rotation on parent 70, turning it into Case 3).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, which case will be invoked when we insert node 10 into the tree?

Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

(Refer to the same tree structure as Question 1)
questions.explanation: Node 10 will be inserted as the left child of node 20. Since node 20 is black, no red-black tree properties are violated. Therefore, no fixing cases are invoked.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the height of the tree?

Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

(Refer to the same tree structure as Question 1)
questions.explanation: The height is the number of edges on the longest path from the root. Path: 40 \(\rightarrow\) 60 \(\rightarrow\) 80 \(\rightarrow\) 70. Length = 3 edges.

questions.text: The array \(\{40, 35, 30, 15, 37, 25, 20, 10, 12\}\) is not a max-heap, but the max-heap properties are violated only once. Which two numbers (parent and child) in the array violate the heap properties?
questions.explanation: In a 1-indexed max-heap, parent of index \(j\) is \(j/2\). At index 2 (value 35), children are at index 4 (15) and index 5 (37). Since 35 < 37, the max-heap property is violated by the pair (35, 37).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, list the keys of the nodes using the pre-order traversal.

Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

(Refer to the same tree structure as Question 1)
questions.explanation: Pre-order: Root (40), Left Subtree (20), Right Subtree (60, then its children 50, 80, and 80's child 70). Result: 40, 20, 60, 50, 80, 70.

questions.text:
If \(f(n) = 5\) and \(g(n) = e^{10}\) :

Note: Both are constant functions.
questions.explanation: Both functions are positive constants. All positive constants belong to the same complexity class \(\Theta(1)\). Thus, \(f(n)\) is \(O(g(n))\), \(\Omega(g(n))\), and \(\Theta(g(n))\).

questions.text: Given the array A \(<15, 2, 8, 1, 10, 5, 12, 7>\) what is the resulting array after applying the partition procedure in the Quick sort algorithm one time (using the last element as the pivot):
Given an array A with the following nodes: 10, 5, 20, 15, 30, 40, 50. After applying the max-heapify at node 2 (value 5) the result will be :
questions.explanation:
Max-heapify at index 2 (value 5). Children are index 4 (15) and index 5 (30). 
1. Largest is 30. Swap 5 and 30. Array becomes: \(10, 30, 20, 15, 5, 40, 50\).
2. Index 5 has no children. Process stops.

questions.text: The height \(h(x)\) of node \(x\) compared to its black-height \(bh(x)\) in a red black tree is:
questions.explanation: By RBT properties, the longest path from a node to a leaf is at most twice the shortest path (which is determined by the black height). Thus, \(h \le 2bh\).

questions.text: Let \(T(n)\) be a function obeying the recurrence \(T(n) = 4T(n / 4) + \log n\) , then \(T(n)=\)
questions.explanation: Master Theorem: \(a=4, b=4, f(n)=\log n\). \(n^{\log_4 4} = n^1\). Since \(f(n) = O(n^{1-\epsilon})\), this is Case 1. Result: \(T(n) = \Theta(n)\).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the successor of node 60?

(Refer to the same tree structure as Question 1)
questions.explanation: The successor of a node is the smallest node in its right subtree. The right subtree of 60 contains nodes 80 and 70. The smallest is 70.

questions.text: Given a heap that contains 512 nodes, the height of the heap rooted at level 4 is:
questions.explanation: A heap with 512 nodes has height \(\lfloor \log_2 512 \rfloor = 9\). Nodes at level 9 exist (the 512th node is the first node of level 9). Height at level 4 = \(9 - 4 = 5\).
The outer loop runs \(n\) times. The inner function `CALC(i)` runs \(i/2\) times. Total work is \(\sum_{i=10}^{n-1} i/2\), which is \(O(n^2)\).

questions.text: Which traversal method outputs the data in sorted order in binary search tree:
questions.explanation: Inorder traversal visits Left, Root, then Right. In a BST, this results in values being visited in ascending order.

questions.text: Given the recurrence relation \( T(n) = n^2 + 3 * T(n / 2) \), and using the recursion tree method, what is the number of nodes in the tree at level i=4?
questions.explanation: Each node splits into \(a=3\) children. At level \(i\), there are \(a^i\) nodes. For \(i=4\), nodes = \(3^4 = 81\).

questions.text: A complete binary search tree has a worst case height of:
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree is balanced by definition. Its height is always logarithmic relative to the number of nodes.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the black height (bh) of node 60?

(Refer to the same tree structure as Question 1)
questions.explanation:
Black height is the number of black nodes from the node to a leaf (excluding itself, including NIL). From 60 (Red):
- To NIL via 50: 50(Black) + NIL(Black) = 2.
- To NIL via 80: 80(Black) + NIL(Black) = 2.
Wait, by definition bh counts black nodes *on the path*. The path 60->50->NIL has 2 black nodes. bh is 2.

questions.text: Insert the following elements into an initially empty binary max-heap: \( \langle 5, 10, 3, 8, 1, 12, 7 \rangle \). What data element is sitting in the left child of the root?
questions.explanation:
To find the element in the left child of the root, we perform step-by-step insertions and heapify (bubble up) operations:

1.  **Insert 5**: `[5]`
2.  **Insert 10**: `[5, 10]` \( \rightarrow \) 10 is greater than 5, swap: `[10, 5]`
3.  **Insert 3**: `[10, 5, 3]`
4.  **Insert 8**: `[10, 5, 3, 8]` \( \rightarrow \) 8 is greater than parent 5, swap: `[10, 8, 3, 5]`
5.  **Insert 1**: `[10, 8, 3, 5, 1]`
6.  **Insert 12**: `[10, 8, 3, 5, 1, 12]` \( \rightarrow \) 12 is greater than parent 3, swap: `[10, 8, 12, 5, 1, 3]` \( \rightarrow \) 12 is greater than parent 10, swap: `[12, 8, 10, 5, 1, 3]`
7.  **Insert 7**: `[12, 8, 10, 5, 1, 3, 7]` \( \rightarrow \) 7 is less than parent 10, no swap.

The final heap array is `[12, 8, 10, 5, 1, 3, 7]`.
- **Root** (Index 0): 12
- **Left Child** (Index 1): 8
- **Right Child** (Index 2): 10

Thus, the element in the left child of the root is 8.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, answer the following questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
  node100((100))
  node50((50))
  node150((150))
  node25((25))
  node75((75))
  node10((10))
  node100 --> node50
  node100 --> node150
  node50 --> node25
  node50 --> node75
  node25 --> node10
  style node100 fill:#000,color:#fff
  style node150 fill:#000,color:#fff
  style node50 fill:#fff,stroke:#f00,color:#f00
  style node25 fill:#000,color:#fff
  style node75 fill:#000,color:#fff
  style node10 fill:#fff,stroke:#f00,color:#f00
```

Which case will be invoked when we insert node 5 into the tree?
questions.explanation: Node 5 is inserted as the left child of 10. 10 is red, 5 is red. Uncle of 10 is the right child of 25 (NIL/Black). Since it's a straight line (Left-Left), Case 3 is invoked.

questions.text: Based on the tree in Q21, which case will be invoked when we insert node 200 into the tree?
questions.explanation: Node 200 is inserted as the right child of 150. Node 150 is black, so no violation occurs.

questions.text: Based on the tree in Q21, what is the height of the tree?
questions.explanation: Longest path is 100 -> 50 -> 25 -> 10, which has 3 edges.

questions.text: List the keys of the nodes in the tree from Q21 using the pre-order traversal.
questions.explanation: Pre-order: 100 (Root), 50 (Left), 25 (Left of 50), 10 (Left of 25), 75 (Right of 50), 150 (Right of 100).

questions.text: What is the successor of node 50 in the tree from Q21?
questions.explanation: The successor is the smallest node in the right subtree. Subtree of 50's right child (75) is just 75.

questions.text: What is the black height (bh) of node 50 in the tree from Q21?
questions.explanation:
Paths from 50 (excluding 50):
- Path to NIL via 75: 75(Black) + NIL(Black) = 2.
- Path to NIL via 25: 25(Black) + 10(Red) + NIL(Black) = 2.

questions.text: What is the depth of node 10 in the tree from Q21?
questions.explanation: Path from 100 to 10: 100->50, 50->25, 25->10. Total 3 edges.
Constant functions are asymptotically equivalent.

questions.text: Given the array A \( <8, 3, 9, 1, 5> \) what is the resulting array after applying the partition procedure in the Quick sort algorithm one time (using the last element 5 as the pivot):
questions.explanation:
Lomuto partition with pivot 5:
- 8 > 5
- 3 < 5 (swap with 8): [3, 8, 9, 1, 5]
- 9 > 5
- 1 < 5 (swap with 8): [3, 1, 9, 8, 5]
- Final swap pivot with 9: [3, 1, 5, 8, 9].

questions.text: Let \( T(n) \) be a function obeying the recurrence \( T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \), then \( T(n) = \)
questions.explanation: Master Theorem: \( a=2, b=2, f(n)=n \). \( n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 \). Since \( f(n) = \Theta(n) \), this is Case 2. Result: \( T(n) = \Theta(n \log n) \).
